skill.name: notebooklm
skill.description: Automate authenticated Google NotebookLM workflows for notebooks, sources, questions, research, and generated study artifacts.
skill.summary: Control Google NotebookLM from an AI assistant with CLI-guided research and artifact workflows.
skill.author: teng-lin

security_audit.summary: Static command, network, filesystem, and crypto alerts mostly come from Markdown examples and an evaluation JSON file, not executable code. The real risk is medium because the skill instructs agents to run an authenticated NotebookLM CLI, send sources to Google services, handle auth state, and download artifacts.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated External CLI Automation
medium_findings.description: The skill directs agents to run many notebooklm commands, including create, ask, source add, generate, wait, and delete. This is legitimate for the skill but can modify cloud data through an authenticated Google account.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit documented behavior, not hidden code execution. Risk remains because an agent may perform account-backed operations if user intent is broad or ambiguous.

medium_findings.title: Credential and Session State Handling
medium_findings.description: The skill documents Google OAuth login, NOTEBOOKLM_AUTH_JSON, storage_state.json, and a default hidden config directory. These items may contain account session data and need careful handling.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential paths and environment variable are directly documented. I found no exfiltration logic, but exposure of these values would be sensitive.

medium_findings.title: Network-Backed Source Import and Research
medium_findings.description: The skill sends URLs, YouTube links, web research queries, and Google Drive searches through NotebookLM workflows. This can disclose source choices and research topics to external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network activity is the advertised function of the skill. It is not malicious, but it affects privacy and data governance.

medium_findings.title: Local Artifact Downloads and File Writes
medium_findings.description: The skill supports downloading generated audio and video artifacts to local paths. It asks for confirmation, but writes can still affect the filesystem.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem write behavior is explicitly documented and scoped to downloads. Confirmation guidance lowers the risk, so this is not high severity.

medium_findings.title: Background Agent Automation for Long Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends spawning background agents that wait for completion and may download artifacts after generation. This increases unattended execution risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subagent prompts are examples for legitimate long tasks. The risk is operational because work can continue outside the main conversation.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Most Ruby or shell backtick alerts are Markdown code spans and fenced examples. They are not executable Ruby backticks in a runtime file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text appears in Markdown documentation and JSON strings. No runnable Ruby, shell script, or source file is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak algorithm flags point to metadata, command tables, generated evaluation text, and limitation prose. I found no cryptographic implementation or hash choice.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not implement MD5, SHA1, DES, or another weak algorithm. They are documentation or generated audit text.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Benign Documentation
low_findings.description: System reconnaissance alerts refer to setup checks, UUID guidance, status checks, and polling intervals. They do not collect host information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines describe NotebookLM workflow state, not operating system reconnaissance. I found no commands like uname, whoami, ifconfig, or process enumeration.

low_findings.title: Evaluation File Is Not Runtime Code
low_findings.description: Several static findings reference EVALUATION.json. That file is a prior review artifact containing quoted examples, not code that the skill executes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is JSON content with audit and marketplace fields. Its flagged strings are descriptions and examples, not executable behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Workflow Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill teaches the assistant to execute notebooklm CLI commands for account-backed actions. This pattern requires user intent checks and confirmation for destructive or write operations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documented commands are real operational instructions. They are not hidden, but they are security-relevant because they cause external side effects.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential-Bearing Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill references NOTEBOOKLM_AUTH_JSON, storage_state.json, and ~/.notebooklm. These storage locations can contain session data for Google services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive storage paths are stated directly. I found no misuse, but marketplace users should understand the credential handling risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Import and Local Download Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill combines source import from URLs and Google services with local artifact downloads. This pattern touches both external services and the local filesystem.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is core functionality and documented clearly. It is dangerous only if sources, queries, or output paths are not reviewed.

content.value_statement: Research teams spend time moving sources into NotebookLM and waiting for generated artifacts. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through repeatable NotebookLM workflows.
actual_capabilities: Checks NotebookLM authentication and notebook context before work starts.
Creates notebooks and adds URL, PDF, YouTube, Google Docs, and text sources.
Asks questions against notebook sources and requests cited answers.
Starts audio, video, quiz, flashcard, slide, and infographic generation.
Waits for source processing, research tasks, and generated artifacts.
Downloads completed audio, video, slide, and infographic artifacts after confirmation.
limitations: Requires a working notebooklm CLI and Google OAuth session.
Generated artifacts may fail because of Google rate limits.
Long generation tasks can take from minutes to almost an hour.
The skill documents workflows but does not include executable code.

use_cases.title: Synthesize Source Collections
use_cases.description: Create a notebook, import source material, and ask grounded questions across the collection.

use_cases.title: Create Study Materials
use_cases.description: Turn course documents into quizzes, flashcards, study guides, and audio overviews.

use_cases.title: Generate Review Artifacts
use_cases.description: Produce NotebookLM podcasts, videos, slides, and infographics from approved source sets.

prompt_templates.title: Check NotebookLM Access
prompt_templates.prompt: Use NotebookLM to check my authentication status and list my notebooks. Stop if login is required.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Research Notebook
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a NotebookLM notebook named {project}. Add these sources: {sources}. Tell me which sources are ready.

prompt_templates.title: Ask Across Sources
prompt_templates.prompt: Use NotebookLM notebook {notebook_id}. Answer {question} using cited source evidence and note any weak evidence.

prompt_templates.title: Generate and Download Artifact
prompt_templates.prompt: Use NotebookLM notebook {notebook_id} to generate {artifact_type} with this focus: {instructions}. Wait in the background and ask before downloading.

output_examples.input: Create a notebook for these policy papers and summarize the main disagreements.
output: Notebook created for the policy papers.
All provided sources were added and processed.
The answer compares the main disagreements and cites the strongest supporting sources.

output_examples.input: Generate an audio overview from my research notebook.
output: The audio overview generation was started.
The artifact is still processing, so the assistant will wait outside the main conversation.
Download will require confirmation after the artifact completes.

output_examples.input: Turn these lecture notes into study materials.
output: The notes were added to a NotebookLM notebook.
The assistant requested quiz and flashcard generation.
Rate limit failures will be reported with retry guidance.

best_practices: Use explicit notebook IDs in parallel workflows to avoid shared context conflicts.
Confirm destructive actions, long generation jobs, and downloads before running them.
Keep NotebookLM auth secrets in protected environment variables or isolated config directories.
anti_patterns: Do not add sensitive sources unless the user approves sending them to Google services.
Do not rely on shared notebook context when multiple agents run at the same time.
Do not download artifacts to broad or unclear filesystem paths.

faq.question: What does this skill automate?
faq.answer: It guides AI assistants through NotebookLM notebook creation, source import, question answering, research, generation, waiting, and downloads.

faq.question: Does this skill include executable code?
faq.answer: No. It is a documentation skill that tells the assistant how to use an installed notebooklm CLI.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: It uses authenticated external commands, Google network services, auth state, and local downloads. These actions are legitimate but sensitive.

faq.question: What must be installed first?
faq.answer: The notebooklm CLI must be available, and the user must complete Google OAuth login.

faq.question: Can it run in automated environments?
faq.answer: Yes, if auth state is provided securely through NOTEBOOKLM_AUTH_JSON or an isolated NOTEBOOKLM_HOME directory.

faq.question: What should users review before running it?
faq.answer: Users should review source sensitivity, notebook IDs, generation requests, and download paths before approving operations.
